turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in No. Ill Original, the State of Delaware v. the State of New York. Mr. Lyons.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court: This case started as a sort of a collection case brought in this Court by Delaware against New York to enforce Delaware's rights under the clear teachings of the backup rule promulgated by this Court in the Texas case in the mid-1960's and reaffirmed by this Court in the Pennsylvania case in the early 1970's. The property in question was so-called overages. These occur when an issuer of securities pays a dividend to its holders of record and you have a broker or some other party that holds for customers or clients as a holder of record, and what the issuer pays is more than what the broker owes to its customers. It also occurs when the brokers are paid by the Depository Trust Company, which we will call the DTC, which is a nominee custodian set up by a cadre of brokers and custodian banks. The property is owed here to unknowns. That is the agreed position of 49 of the States except for New York, which says that it may be owed to knowns, but it can't identify who they are at this point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why does this occur so often and generate so much revenue?

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: It occurs, I believe, because of the activities in the securities markets, that you have an issuer who's maintaining a record, you have securities which are traded, and they sometimes get traded in semibearer form. In other words, a stock certificate comes out, it is endorsed and negotiable in blank form, and it passed from hand to hand, and the issuer, of course, doesn't know about this. It pays the record, and that results in someone being overpaid and someone being underpaid, and I think it has to do with the volume of trading and the fact that we have a stock-certificate-based trading system in most areas. This does not involve the primary rule. The case is narrowly drawn simply to involve the backup rule. The primary rule, of course, involves lost stockholders, people who are on the record but who the issuer can't find, who the issuer has addresses for but has lost touch with, and lost customers of brokers, and we estimate in our brief - - there is no evidence in the record - - that the universe of the primary rule here, the escheats, is much larger than that of the backup rule. What started as a collection case turned into a stampede. One by one, 48 States intervened, and they came up with a position that was different from the positions that were quite common between the plaintiff and the defendant in this case. The effect of these positions was to enlarge the size of the universe that was in issue in the case. The property that Delaware sought was the property in the hands of the brokers who were incorporated in Delaware. The property that the intervenors sought for themselves was the property in the hands of the DTC, a very considerable amount of property, the property in the hands of the Delaware brokers, the property in the hands of the brokers incorporated elsewhere, and the property in the hands of the New York custodian banks, which make a specialty upholding securities for customers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Lyons, if we were to apply the rule and the backup rule and the doctrines enunciated by this Court in prior cases, does that mean Delaware would prevail here?

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Delaware would prevail --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, it's the State of incorporation of the holder - -

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of the dividends.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Yes, it would, and New York would prevail as to the - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Treating the holder as the creditor - -

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: As the debtor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Debtor.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: As the debtor, yes, Your Honor. That is our position, that the debtor here is the broker, is the DTC, but in particular, since the DT doesn't concern us - - New York gets that under our theory - - that the broker is the debtor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well now, the Special Master didn't take that view and didn't treat the broker as the debtor.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: He came close to acknowledging, if Your Honor please, that the broker was a debtor under State law, and he said the State law was technical and that what we needed here was a Federal common law, and under Federal common law, even though it wasn't the case under State law, and I think that's quite clear -- there's a litany of reasons why it's clear --we will treat the issuer as being the debtor. He had a little process whereby he teased out ambiguities, and after he finished teasing the ambiguities he had created an ambiguity, and he resolved it with a tie-breaker, in a sports analogy, and the tie-breaker was fairness, and the principle of fairness was to send the money back where it came from, and the money, he said, came from the issuer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Like in the Western Union case.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Beg pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Like in the Western Union case.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: No, that was the decision of Congress in a very narrowly drafted statute, it was not the decision of this Court. This Court in the very conservative decision by Justice Brennan literally applied the teachings of the Texas case to that matter, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what was Western Union to do when it couldn't find the payee, the money -- the person to whom the money was sent?

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Western Union couldn't find either the payee - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well - -

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Or the sender.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if they couldn't find the payee?

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Well, the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then what did they do?

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: The sender was still the -- was still a creditor in that case, and if they had an address for the creditor they were to escheat it to the sender as the creditor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'm not sure you could call the sender necessarily a creditor.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Well, he's given the money to the Western Union.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Like his son, or something like that, and they couldn't find the son.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Well, he's given the money to the Western Union. If the Western Union can't find his son, then on principles of equity it is clear under State law that Western Union can't keep the money, that they have to give it back to the sender.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, certainly these broker intermediaries didn't have any real claim to the funds themselves.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: Oh, they do in this sense. They do not owe it back to the issuers. It is quite clear that they -- if they had to pay it back to the issuer --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they don't -- they -- if they could find --

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: That --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that may be but if they could find the ultimate beneficiary they were supposed to send it on.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: They're supposed to give it to the beneficiary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So they didn't really have any title to it.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: But they -- in the meantime they were earning the interest on it

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May be. May be.

turns.name: DENNIS G. LYONS
turns.text: They're using the money in their business. Most -- that is, of course, a chronic position of a debtor in an escheat case, that in all these cases by definition the debtor in an escheat case owes the money to somebody else, but if that person cannot be found within the period of latency, then you have an escheat, so I think simply to say that the debtor owes the money to someone else doesn't resolve the question. It's the start of the question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Lyons. We'll resume there at 1:00 p.m.
